faculty.name: Ahmed El-Gendy
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. El-Gendy received his Ph.D. degree in physics from Heidelberg University, Germany (2008-2011). From 2012-2017, He served in various research positions on both levels academia and industry, as a postdoctoral researcher and senior scientist at Virginia Commonwealth University (VCU), Virginia (2012-2013, 2016-2017), University of Delaware (UD), Delaware (2013-2015), Nanofoundary LLC. (2015-2016), USA. In fall 2017, he joined department of physics at UTEP as tenure-track assistant professor to establish his group in nanomagnetics and biomaterials (Nanoland). El-Gendy has been working in the field of magnetic nanoparticles for 20 years. His research expertise includes synthesis, characterization and applications of functionalized magnetic nanoparticles. He focuses on synthesis and optimization of soft and hard magnetic nanomaterials using chemical and physical methods to be used for various types of applications such as hyperthermia treatment for cancer, drug delivery, contrast agent for MRI, rare-earth free permanent magnets, data storage, and magneto-caloric for refrigeration technology. In 2020 and 2023, El-Gendy was awarded NSF grants to organize the 1st and 2nd US-North Africa Conference of Nanotechnology convergence for health, energy and environment. Since 2021, El-Gendy is collaborating with MD Anderson cancer center on developing new alternative cancer therapy (ranked top 1 in cancer research). El-Gendy is the recipient of the IAAM Scientist Medal 2023 and Advanced Materials Awards 2024 by IAAM, Sweden. During his career, El-Gendy secured external funds to support his research in particular his novel cancer therapy approach that is being performed now in his Nanoland Lab.

faculty.name: Alex Mayer
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Alex Mayer is Professor of Civil, Environmental, and Construction Engineering at the University of Texas at El Paso, with over 40 years of experience in environmental and water resources engineering. Dr. Mayer’s teaching and research focuses on finding collaborative solutions to environmental problems through modeling and interactions with stakeholders. His current research topics include water resources systems modeling, transboundary aquifer governance, impacts of sea level rise on coastal groundwater aquifers, water access in underserved populations, climate change impacts on water resources, urban heat islands, and the role of trees in desert cities. His research has taken place in the Rio Grande basin, Caribbean, Laurentian Great Lakes, and Sonora and Veracruz, Mexico. He has directed over $12 million in externally-funded research and published 140 refereed papers and book chapters.

faculty.name: Anantaa Kotal
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio: Anantaa Kotal is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at UTEP, where she leads the PRISM Lab (Private, Responsible, and Interpretable Systems in Machine Learning). Her research centers on building trustworthy and accountable AI systems that operate safely in human-centered and expertise-driven domains. Dr. Kotal advances the foundations of Trustworthy and Generative AI, developing frameworks that align algorithmic innovation with ethical responsibility. Her work addresses privacy in data use, fairness in model inference, and explainability in decision processes, aiming to make AI systems both useful and justifiable—capable of integrating scientific and domain knowledge with data-driven learning. She also explores policy-aligned AI, designing computational models that embed legal and governance constraints directly into their architecture. Her interdisciplinary research spans computing, healthcare, cybersecurity, and the natural and physical sciences, uniting machine learning with scientific discovery, data ethics, and institutional trust to enable AI systems that are as responsible as they are intelligent. Dr. Kotal’s research appears in venues such as IEEE Big Data, ACM CIKM, AMIA, and IEEE Internet Computing, supported by the USDOT and the UT System Trauma Research Collaborative.

faculty.name: Aritran Piplai
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Aritran Piplai is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His research focuses on understanding how evolving data can inform the design of intelligent systems that are robust, adaptive, and generalizable within the dynamic landscape of cybersecurity. As cyber threats continuously change in form, scale, and intent, developing AI systems that can learn, reason, and generalize beyond static training data is critical. My work explores how AI, particularly large language models (LLMs), neuro-symbolic reasoning, and reinforcement learning, can contribute to building such systems. In this context, I study how LLMs can be adapted and aligned for cybersecurity applications, such as automated threat detection, cyber reasoning, and defense strategy generation. Another important direction of my research involves neuro-symbolic approaches that combine the statistical strengths of deep learning with the logical rigor of symbolic reasoning, enabling models to not only detect anomalies but also explain their reasoning processes in human-interpretable terms. I also investigate the use of reinforcement learning for developing adaptive defense mechanisms that evolve through interaction with simulated or real cyber environments, allowing security policies to improve over time. By integrating these approaches, my long-term vision is to advance the foundations of intelligent, trustworthy, and explainable AI systems capable of responding effectively. This involves bridging the gap between machine learning, human reasoning, and real-world constraints to create systems that can anticipate, adapt to, and mitigate threats in an ever-changing digital ecosystem.

faculty.name: Arshad Khan
faculty.title:
Professor 
 Department: Biological Sciences
faculty.bio: Dr. Khan directs the UTEP Systems Neuroscience Laboratory, where he and his students are focused on mapping brain circuits that control hunger, craving and nutrient sensing. As a neuroscientist whose research interests straddle the domains of neuroanatomy, neurochemistry, cartography, computer science, and behavior, he collaborates widely with domain experts across all these disciplines. As an affiliated faculty member of the AAII, he is interested in the use of computer vision to help guide his team in mapping brain circuits, and the promise of spatial AI to streamline their investigation. His work involves the careful annotation and standardization of these circuits to open-access brain atlases to help researchers target these circuits to develop better therapies for neurological illness and disease. More fundamentally, work from his team could help us better understand how the brain is organized into functional systems. He envisions AI to aid in not only the spatial mapping of the brain, but also in hypothesis generation, experimental design, and data integration for experiments involving brain circuits, as well as a resource for trainees as they develop their research projects.

faculty.name: Chad Hoyer
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
 Department: Chemistry
faculty.bio: Dr. Chad Hoyer is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemistry at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). He received his PhD in Chemical Physics from the University of Minnesota under the supervision of Donald Truhlar and Laura Gagliardi and followed up with a postdoc at University of Washington under Xiaosong Li. His expertise is in the broadly-defined area of computational and theoretical chemistry, specifically the development and application of quantum mechanics to the study of molecules and materials. Chad Hoyer started as Assistant Professor in the chemistry department at UTEP in Fall 2024. His research program is currently tackling problems in nuclear energy, rare-earth separations, catalysis, and quantum computing.
expertise_keywords: University of Texas at El Paso

faculty.name: Chris Kiekintveld
faculty.title: Professor
faculty.bio:
Christopher Kiekintveld is a full professor of computer science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His research in artificial intelligence focuses on addressing the core problems of deploying AI for real-world problems, especially in the areas of security and economics. He is particularly interested in multi-agent systems and robustness, including computational game theory, adversarial learning, decision making under uncertainty, and related areas. He has published more than 100 papers in top CS journals and conferences, and received funding support from agencies including NSF, ARO/ARL, IARPA, DHS, and ONR. He has worked on several deployed applications of AI including systems for FAMS and TSA, and has received many best paper awards, the David Rist Prize, and an NSF CAREER Award. He is particularly proud of the many students he has supervised and the contributions they continue to make in advancing the future of AI research for social good. faculty.name: Deepak Tosh
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.bio:
Dr. Deepak Tosh is an Associate Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His research interests span cybersecurity of critical infrastructure, data provenance, trust and resiliency of industrial networks, cyber-threat information sharing, cyber-risk monitoring and assessment, and the use of game theory and mechanism design to enhance system security. Motivated by the growing challenges of trust and security in networked systems, the overarching goal of his research is to develop resilient and sustainable mechanisms that address various complex cyber and data security challenges associated to modern cyber-physical system environments. As communities become increasingly interconnected while emerging cyber-threats tend to be advanced, he focuses on designing secure, decentralized, and scalable solutions to strengthen both civilian applications (e.g. Internet of Things, Cloud and Edge computing platforms) and military/battlefield environments. His research has been supported by grants from National Science Foundation (NSF), Department of Energy (DOE), and Department of Defense (DoD). He is a recipient of prestigious NSF CAREER Award. Chemistry and Computer Science Bldg. (CCSB), Room 3.1010  
 915-747-6410  
 dktosh@utep.edu

faculty.name: Fei Xu
faculty.title: Regents’ Associate Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Xu is an Associate Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at EL Paso. She obtained her Ph.D. in Computer Science from Utah State University in 2020. Dr. Xu worked as research scientist in Idaho National Laboratory (INL) from 2021 to 2025. She has led and contributed to over 10 funded projects, driving innovation at the intersection of AI and critical applications. Her excellence has been consistently recognized with prestigious honors, including the Gold Medal for postdoctoral researchers at INL in 2022 and the Best Mentor Award at INL in 2023. Dr. Xu’s research interests include developing AI models for high-fidelity and scarce data analysis, and generative AI approaches for image synthesis—capabilities critical to fields like healthcare, nuclear energy, critical infrastructure protection, and advanced manufacturing. She pioneered the development of hybrid models for the early detection of breast cancer in ultrasound images, innovatively integrating human visual mechanisms and anatomical knowledge to improve diagnostic performance. As an active contributor to the scientific community, Dr. Xu has a prolific publication record and serves as a guest editor, conference co-organizer, and reviewer for leading journals and conferences, consistently advancing the fields of AI and its practical applications.
expertise_keywords: Nuclear Material and Medical Science Data Analysis

faculty.name: Hernan Moreno
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Hernán A. Moreno is an Associate Professor in the Department of Earth, Environmental and Resource Sciences at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His recent research focuses on Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KANs) for interpretable physics discovery, AI-driven soil moisture estimation, and anomaly detection in satellite precipitation records, with applications spanning water resources, land-atmosphere interactions, and critical mineral exploration. He leads and collaborates on projects supported by NSF, NOAA, NASA, USDA, DOE, and ARO, advancing the role of explainable AI in Earth system modeling. At UTEP, he teaches courses on Machine Learning in Geoscience, GeoAI and Advanced GIST, and AI: Geospatial Models and Technologies, fostering experiential learning and AI literacy in the geosciences. Dr. Moreno is also actively involved in curriculum innovation, outreach, and student research development, including the annual Geo-X Day competition supported by the White Award Endowment. His broader mission is to enable responsible and transparent AI for Earth and environmental sciences, bridging data-driven discovery with physical understanding to address challenges in climate, water, and sustainability.

faculty.name: Hugo Escalante
faculty.title: Distinguished Regents’ Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Hugo Jair Escalante will join the Department of Computer Science at The University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP) in 2026 as a Distinguished Regents Professor. He currently serves on the Board of Directors of ChaLearn, an international organization advancing machine learning through open scientific challenges, and as Vice-Chair of the IAPR Technical Committee 12 on Multimedia and Visual Information Systems. He is also a member of the Mexican Academy of Sciences and a Level II member of the Mexican National System of Researchers (SNII). Dr. Escalante’s research focuses on challenge organization and machine learning, with applications in language and vision. He has published more than 200 peer-reviewed papers, cited over 9,000 times, and has received several best paper and thesis awards at international conferences. He previously served as Editor-in-Chief of the Springer Series on Challenges in Machine Learning (2017–2023) and is currently an Associate Editor for IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing and the Data-Centric Machine Learning Research Journal. Dr. Escalante has organized and chaired numerous AI competitions and workshops, including NeurIPS 2019–2020, FG 2020, and ICPR 2020, and continues to contribute to leading venues such as CVPR and ICCV. He was recognized among the Top 6 Researchers in Computer Science in Mexico (Research.com, 2022–2025).

faculty.name: Jaime Acosta
faculty.title: Dr. Jaime Acosta is a Professor and the Army Research Laboratory (ARL) South Site Lead at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP), where he also serves as an affiliated faculty member in the Department of Computer Science. He earned his Ph.D. in Computer Science from UTEP in 2009.
faculty.bio: Dr. Jaime Acosta is a Professor and the Army Research Laboratory (ARL) South Site Lead at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP), where he also serves as an affiliated faculty member in the Department of Computer Science. He earned his Ph.D. in Computer Science from UTEP in 2009. His work centers on advancing cybersecurity research and innovation through collaboration with faculty, federal agencies, and industry partners. Dr. Acosta leads efforts to address complex cyber challenges by organizing and engaging in innovation exercises, hackathons, and beyond–state-of-the-art research initiatives that foster real-world problem solving and interdisciplinary collaboration. With a focus on bridging academic research and applied defense technology, his work contributes to developing innovative cybersecurity solutions that enhance system resilience and operational readiness.

faculty.name: Jorge Munoz
faculty.title:
Associate Professor 
 Department: Physics
faculty.bio: Jorge Munoz is an Associate Professor of Physics at The University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His research group develops computational methodology, machine learning algorithms, and data pipelines. The group consists of computationally-inclined physicists, mathematicians, and engineers who work on understanding the structure, thermodynamics, and properties of ordinary and nuclear matter from as close as reasonably possible to quantum mechanics. The work is done on Department of Energy (DOE) and National Science Foundation (NSF) supercomputers. Jorge received a PhD in Materials Physics from Caltech and a BS with a double major in Applied Mathematics and Physics from UTEP. He was a recipient of the Gates Millennium Scholarship and was named as a 2022 Cottrell Scholar the by Research Corporation for Science Advancement. He worked at Intel Corporation as an Engineer in Computer-Aided Design and as a Data Scientist in Algorithms Pathfinding. At Intel, he invented a machine learning algorithm based on the information entropy of mathematical graphs that was patented and has been influential. Jorge has over 40 publications in peer-reviewed journals and has been granted 3 US patents. He has received research grants as a Principal Investigator at UTEP totaling more than 1 million dollars, and significant resources from Berkeley Lab in direct student support. He cares deeply about mentoring, and alumni of his group can now be found at many top academic institutions and tech companies. He is a faculty affiliate at Berkeley Lab, UTEP Applied AI Innovation Institute, and UTEP Computational Science Program. Other Affiliations: Berkeley Lab (Center for Computational Science and Engineering)

faculty.name: Kuldeep Singh
faculty.title: Associate Professor of Instruction
faculty.bio:
Dr. Kuldeep Singh is an Assistant Professor of Instruction in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). He earned his Ph.D. in Computer Science from the Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology in India in 2018 and previously served as a Postdoctoral Fellow at Carnegie Mellon University, where he contributed to research on social engineering and cyber deception. His research explores the cognitive, social, and environmental factors that influence cybersecurity behavior, with specific focus on phishing, cyberbullying, and campus safety. His broader interests include behavioral cybersecurity, privacy, and agent-based modeling, which he applies to study human interaction and decision-making in digital environments. As both a researcher and educator, Dr. Singh is dedicated to bridging theory and practice by connecting academic research in cybersecurity with real-world applications. He is a published scholar and active journal reviewer, committed to mentoring students and promoting awareness and resilience against evolving cyber threats. Other Affiliations:  
 Keywords of Expertise: Behavioral Cybersecurity, Privacy, Cyber Deception, Agent-Based Modeling, Social Engineering

faculty.name: Laura Alvarez
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
 Department: Earth, Environmental and Resource Sciences
faculty.bio: Dr. Laura V. Alvarez is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Earth, Environmental and Resource Sciences at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP), where she directs the GeoSenSE Lab (Geospatial Sensing and Environmental Science Engineering). Her research integrates computational fluid dynamics (CFD), machine learning, and field robotics to advance understanding of fluvial processes, sediment transport, and environmental sensing. Dr. Alvarez is a National Science Foundation CAREER Award recipient for her project “Understanding the Physics of Turbulent Flow, Erosion, and Depositional Patterns in River Systems.” Her research program combines high-resolution numerical modeling and autonomous systems to investigate river morphodynamics and develop predictive frameworks for environmental change. She has secured multiple grants from NSF, NOAA, and the U.S. Army Research Office. At UTEP, Dr. Alvarez teaches across the GeoAI and Physical Geography curriculum, mentoring undergraduate, graduate, and postdoctoral researchers in computational geoscience, remote sensing, and environmental data science. She is deeply committed to broadening participation in STEM through inclusive mentoring and outreach initiatives such as Earth Science Day and NOAA’s CESSRST and IMAGEN programs. Her scholarly work appears in leading journals, including Earth Surface Processes and Landforms and Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface. Through her interdisciplinary approach, Dr. Alvarez aims to bridge environmental physics, artificial intelligence, and geospatial technology to improve the prediction and sustainability of riverine and terrestrial systems. As director of the GeoSenSE Lab, she fosters a collaborative, student-centered research environment that emphasizes innovation, mentorship, and diversity in the geosciences.

faculty.name: Lela Vukovic
faculty.title: Dr. Lela Vukovic is an Associate Professor in the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at The University of Texas at El Paso. Her research focuses on computational chemistry approaches to understand molecular recognition at biomolecule-nanomaterial interfaces, with applications in biosensing and biomedical nanotechnology. She has published broadly in computational and biophysical chemistry and her work has been supported by the National Science Foundation and the National Institutes of Health. Dr. Vukovic is also a Humboldt Research Fellow and the recipient of the UTEP Distinguished Faculty Award.
faculty.bio: Dr. Lela Vukovic is an Associate Professor in the Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry at The University of Texas at El Paso. Her research focuses on computational chemistry approaches to understand molecular recognition at biomolecule-nanomaterial interfaces, with applications in biosensing and biomedical nanotechnology. She has published broadly in computational and biophysical chemistry and her work has been supported by the National Science Foundation and the National Institutes of Health. Dr. Vukovic is also a Humboldt Research Fellow and the recipient of the UTEP Distinguished Faculty Award. Her teaching spans undergraduate and graduate courses in physical and computational chemistry, where she emphasizes student success in mathematically and conceptually demanding material. As an advocate for curriculum innovation, Dr. Vukovic has introduced hands-on modules in artificial intelligence and machine learning into the undergraduate chemistry curriculum, funded by the Academy of Distinguished Teachers grant, created a graduate course on scientific writing and communication, and is developing new materials and approaches to support student preparation in physical chemistry. She mentors undergraduate and graduate researchers, who have gone on to earn awards and positions in academia and industry. Her mission in the classroom is to combine rigor with support, preparing students to thrive in their academic and professional careers. Prior to her time at UTEP, Dr. Vukovic completed her B.Sc. and Ph.D. in Chemistry from the University of Illinois at Chicago, followed by research at the Max Planck Institute for Biophysical Chemistry and postdoctoral work at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. Other Affiliations: Computational Science Program (UTEP), Bioinformatics Program (UTEP), College of Science

faculty.name: Marcelo Frias
faculty.title:
Professor 
 Department: Department of Computer Science
faculty.bio:
Marcelo Frias is a Professor of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso. His research focuses in sound automated software engineering, i.e., software engineering tools and techniques whose soundness and or completeness can be guaranteed. He publishes in conferences such as ICSE, ASE, FSE, ISSTA and journals like ACM TOSEM or IEEE TSE. In his current teaching is combining foundations of programming and generative AI to teach students how to generate provably correct code. HE is the director of the FASE Lab (Foundational Aspects of Software Engineering) where he works with his undergraduate and graduate research assistants. faculty.name: Md Fashiar Rahman
faculty.title: Assistant Professor
faculty.bio: Dr. Md Fashiar Rahman is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Industrial, Manufacturing, and Systems Engineering (IMSE) at The University of Texas at El Paso. He earned his Ph.D. (2021) and M.S. (2018) in Computational Science, with a strong research background in image data mining, machine learning, and deep learning, particularly in industrial and healthcare applications. His expertise spans data analytics, computer modeling and simulation, artificial intelligence in healthcare, smart manufacturing, computational intelligence, and advanced quality technology. Dr. Rahman’s research has received funding from various prestigious agencies, including the National Science Foundation (NSF), the U.S. Department of Education, and Lockheed Martin. His scholarly work has been widely published in peer-reviewed journals and conferences, including Biomedical Signal Processing and Control, Diagnostics, Journal of Systems Science, Journal of X-Ray Science and Technology, Journal of Visual Communication and Image Representation, CIRP Manufacturing Letters, Winter Simulation Conference, ASEE, and IISE Annual Conference and Expo, among others. Beyond his research and academic contributions, Dr. Rahman has played an active leadership role in the professional community. He has served as Secretary, Program Chair, and Division Chair of the Manufacturing Division of the American Society for Engineering Education (ASEE), further demonstrating his commitment to advancing engineering education and research.
expertise_keywords: University of Texas at El Paso

faculty.name: Monika Akbar
faculty.title: Associate Professor
faculty.bio:
Dr. Monika Akbar is an Associate Professor of Computer Science at The University of Texas at El Paso and an affiliated faculty member of the Institute for Applied AI Innovation (AAII). Her research spans information retrieval, data integration, and analytics, with applications in cybersecurity and public health. She develops AI- and ML-driven models to identify and analyze vulnerabilities, anticipate emerging threats, and strengthen the resilience of critical systems. Her recent work integrates structured and unstructured cybersecurity datasets such as, CVE, CWE, and MITRE ATT&CK, using graph-based and language-model techniques to uncover new associations for proactive defense. Beyond cybersecurity, Dr. Akbar's interdisciplinary research extends to neuro-rehabilitation, where she collaborates with clinical researchers to translate wearable sensor data into interpretable narratives that support recovery assessment after stroke. This work bridges AI, ML, human behavior, and health analytics to make data more meaningful and actionable for patients and practitioners alike. At UTEP, she teaches courses in Cybersecurity Fundamentals and Information Retrieval, and serves as faculty lead for the Miner Cybersecurity Club, fostering applied learning and workforce readiness. In alignment with AAII’s mission to advance real-world AI solutions and regional impact, Dr. Akbar’s work connects artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, and health analytics to inform decision-making and improve lives across diverse domains. faculty.name: Olac Fuentes
faculty.title:
Professor 
 Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio: Dr. Fuentes is a Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP), with additional affiliations in Bioinformatics and Computational Science. He received his Ph.D. in Computer Science from the University of Rochester in 1997, his M.S. in Computer Science from UTEP in 1991, and his B.S. in Industrial Engineering from the Instituto Tecnológico de Chihuahua in Mexico in 1989. His primary research area is machine learning in science, with a focus on developing intelligent systems that help scientists extract knowledge and insight from large volumes of scientific data. His interests include the use of unlabeled data, active learning, intelligent optimization, feature selection and creation, noise-insensitive and noise-aware algorithms, and the integration of prior knowledge into learning systems. Fuentes has applied these methods across diverse fields, including astronomy, optics, geology, and biology. Before joining UTEP, he served as a Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the Instituto Nacional de Astrofísica, Óptica y Electrónica (INAOE) in Mexico from 1998 to 2005. He was also a co-organizer of the First Iberoamerican Workshop on Machine Learning for Scientific Data Analysis, held in conjunction with IBERAMIA 2004 in Puebla, Mexico.

faculty.name: Palvi Aggarwal
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
 Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio:
Dr. Palvi Aggarwal is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso. Her research integrates cybersecurity, human-machine teaming, and cognitive modeling to understand and influence adversarial decision-making in cyber contexts. Before joining UTEP, she was a Postdoctoral Fellow at Carnegie Mellon University’s Dynamic Decision Making Laboratory. Dr. Aggarwal has received multiple research grants from agencies such as IARPA, the Department of Defense, and the National Science Foundation. Her work has earned several Best Paper Awards at international venues including HICSS, GameSec, and AHFE. She is also a recipient of IIT Mandi’s Young Alumni Achiever Award for Academic Excellence. Her research has been published in leading journals such as Computers & Security, Frontiers in Psychology, and Cognitive Science. Chemistry and Computer Science Building, Room: CCSB 3.0406

faculty.name: Saidur Rahman
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
 Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio: Dr. Mohammad Saidur Rahman is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). His research focuses on the intersection of machine learning, cybersecurity, and quantum security, with a primary focus on developing ML-driven intelligent systems for malware and network traffic analysis. Among others, his work explores continual learning frameworks for improved malware detection and classification. His contributions also extend to large-scale malware datasets and binary analysis pipelines, where he combines machine learning with reverse engineering and program analysis to automate feature extraction from binaries and improve resilience against adversarial manipulation. In network security domain, Dr. Rahman examines how machine learning can both reveal and mitigate vulnerabilities in encrypted traffic, improving privacy and resilience. By designing attack and defense models, his work advances the understanding of information leakage and privacy risks in encrypted environments. Furthrmore, Dr. Rahman has led investigations into post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration strategies for IoT devices, evaluating NIST candidate algorithms such as Kyber, BIKE, and HQC. His work also explores quantum key distribution (QKD) for satellite and anonymous communication networks like Tor, addressing real-world integration and security challenges. Dr. Rahman's research contributions have been recognized in premier venues in security and privacy, including IEEE S&P, ACM CCS, PoPETS, and IEEE TIFS, as well as in leading machine learning conferences such as AAAI and CoLLAs, and in quantum communications venues including IEEE QCNC and IEEE QCE. He has been recognized as a rising star in AI and ML for security by the IEEE TCCN SIG on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Security.

faculty.name: Sajedul Talukder
faculty.title:
Assistant Professor 
Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio:
Dr. Sajedul Talukder is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP) and the director of the Security and Privacy Enhanced Machine Learning Lab (SUPREME Lab). His research focuses on security and privacy in online systems, AI/ML, online social networks, cyber-physical and distributed systems, critical infrastructure security, and AI for social good. Dr. Talukder has secured significant funding from leading federal agencies, including the National Science Foundation (NSF), U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), Cyber Florida, and the Florida Center for Cybersecurity. Notable awards include a $500,000 U.S. NRC grant for AI-driven cybersecurity in U.S. nuclear plants, a NSF CISE CRII Award under the Secure and Trustworthy Cyberspace (SaTC) program for AI-based defense against sockpuppet attacks in social networks, and a $99,000 IMEC Award from NIST for cybersecurity integration in industrial IoT environments. He has authored over one hundred (100+) peer-reviewed publications in leading venues such as ACM TSC, Nature Scientific Reports, ANS Transactions, ASONAM, ACM SAC, ICWSM, ICMLA, PoPETS, ACM CHI, ACM HyperText, ACM WebSci, COMPSAC, IEEE DSC, and my research has received media attention from many news outlets including prominent national and international outlets such as NBC, KFOX, CBS, KMOV TV, and Sage Methods. He is an active member of IEEE and ACM. Before joining UTEP, Dr. Talukder held faculty positions at Southern Illinois University Carbondale and Pennsylvania Western University (PennWest), where he received several teaching and research awards including PennWest's Scholar of the Year in 2020 and SIU's University-Level Early Career Faculty Excellence Award in 2022 and 2024. At UTEP, he received the Student Choice High Impact and Innovative Teaching (HIIT) Award (2025) for excellence in teaching and student engagement. He has mentored over 40 students and serves on national panels, editorial boards and program committees for numerous venues including leading journals and conferences. faculty.name: Shahriar Hossain
faculty.title:
Professor and Interim Chair 
 Department: Computer Science
faculty.bio: Dr. Hossain is the Interim Chair and a Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). He earned his Ph.D. in Computer Science from Virginia Tech in 2012 and joined UTEP in 2013. His research specializes in artificial intelligence and deep learning, with interests that include the modeling of evolving data, large language models, and generative models. His work follows a two-pronged approach: on one hand, he develops theoretical and mathematical frameworks for advanced machine learning algorithms; on the other, he applies these methods to address complex, real-world challenges that current AI tools cannot fully resolve. The interaction between his theoretical insights and applied collaborations drives innovation across disciplines. Over the years, Dr. Hossain has collaborated extensively with experts in neurorehabilitation, agriculture, civil engineering, biomedical sciences, health, manufacturing, energy-efficient design, national security, and cybersecurity. These interdisciplinary partnerships have shaped his research philosophy and strengthened his commitment to advancing AI for societal benefit. In addition to his research, Dr. Hossain is passionate about expanding access to computing education. He founded computing4all.com, a platform dedicated to sharing computing, AI, and data science educational materials, complemented by his instructional YouTube channel.

faculty.name: Shirley Moore

faculty.bio:
Dr. Shirley V. Moore is an Associate Professor in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). She received her PhD in Computer Sciences from Purdue University in 1990. Her research areas are in high performance and parallel computing, including parallel and distributed deep learning. She has worked in high performance computing research for 35 years. She has published numerous conference and journal papers in the area and has been involved in developing widely used software for performance measurment and analysis. faculty.name: Alejandra Castellanos
expertise_keywords: PEOPLE CENTERS COMMUNITIES RESEARCH STORIES Expertise Finder
publications: PEOPLE CENTERS COMMUNITIES RESEARCH STORIES Expertise Finder
Associate Professor, Aerospace and Mechanical Engineering
Dr. Alejandra Castellanos is an Assistant Professor at the Aerospace and Mechanical Engineering Department at the
University of Texas of El Paso. She obtained her Ph.D. from the University of Wisconsin-Madison in 2019, and has been
awarded three times the Air Force Research Lab (AFRL) Summer Faculty Fellowship Program (SFFP) award: 2021 (RQ-
Aerospace Systems Directorate, WPAFB), 2022 and 2023 (RX-Materials and Manufacturing Directorate, WPAFB). Her
research has gained international recognition, exemplified by her invitation to participate at the 2023 US-Japan
Symposium Conference and her distinguished selection as an Amelia Earhart Fellow by Zonta International in 2017. Her
research has been funded by AFRL, AFOSR and NSF.Her research has been focused on three main aspects: 1) Enhancing
the damage tolerance of polymer matrix composites (PMC) under quasi-static and dynamic loading through
nanotechnology (vertically aligned carbon nanotubes, Aramid pulp, and ZnO nanowires). Towards that, she has focused
on manufacturing, testing, and developing multiscale computational models to capture damage propagation during impact
loadings. 2) Establishing the processing-structure-property relationship of ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) for
hypersonic applications. She has developed high-fidelity multiphysics computational models to establish how
manufacturing defects are developed and how they impact the CMCs’ thermo-mechanical properties. 3) Developing a
data-driven framework assisted with machine learning (ML) based on entropy-forming ability (EFA) descriptor and Hume-
Rothery size difference factors to discover high-entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics (HE-UHTCs) with exceptional
oxidation and mechanical properties for hypersonic environments.
